planting.note: Plant container divisions or transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; brunnera is a cool-season performer that looks best in spring and early summer, typically going summer-dormant in hot, humid climates.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Siberian bugloss is a spring gem of the shade garden — forming low mounds of large, deep-green, heart-shaped leaves covered from early to mid-spring in tiny, vivid sky-blue flowers that exactly resemble forget-me-not blossoms. The flowers appear before the leaves reach full size, creating an airy blue haze above the emerging foliage. After flowering, the large, textured leaves expand to 8–10 inches wide and provide attractive coverage through early summer. In climates with hot, humid summers, brunnera may go partly summer-dormant, which is normal — the leaves decline but the plant revives in fall. Named cultivars add silver variegation: 'Jack Frost' has entire silver leaves with only green veining, 'Looking Glass' is nearly all-silver, and 'Sea Heart' has silver patterns with dark margins. All cultivars need consistent moisture and protection from afternoon sun to maintain their silver markings. Self-seeds freely in cool, moist shade garden conditions.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Jack Frost cannot be grown from seed (it will not come true) and must be propagated from divisions taken in early spring or late summer.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Jack Frost brunnera was the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2012 and remains the most celebrated and widely grown silver-foliage shade perennial available. The large, heart-shaped leaves are almost entirely covered with a brilliant, reflective silver overlay, with only the green primary veins visible — in the shade garden, the silver leaves appear to glow with an inner light and illuminate dark corners as effectively as white flowers. In spring, tiny azure-blue forget-me-not flowers appear on wiry stems above the silver foliage, creating a jewel-like spring display. The silvery foliage remains effective from emergence in spring through summer decline, brightening the shade border for months. Consistent moisture is the most critical requirement — Jack Frost scorches badly in dry conditions and in hot afternoon sun. In zone 8, afternoon shade, consistent watering, and mulching are required to maintain it through summer. It will not produce viable seeds.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is a native woodland plant that establishes slowly but eventually forms large, permanent clumps that should not be moved once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is one of the most dramatic native shade perennials of eastern North America — a bold woodland plant with large, deeply divided, compound leaves rising from thick rhizomes to form a substantial mound 3–4 feet wide, topped in summer by extraordinary wand-like flower spikes that reach 4–6 feet or more. The white flowers, borne on the tall bottlebrush-like racemes, have a subtle, slightly musky fragrance. The genus was formerly called Cimicifuga (bugbane), reflecting the historical use of the plant to deter insects. The rhizomes are used in traditional medicine and widely sold as an herbal supplement for menopausal symptoms; the plants are being over-collected from wild populations, making garden cultivation an important conservation measure. In the garden it is outstanding under tall trees where its white flowers illuminate the dark understory in midsummer. Purple-leafed cultivars like 'Hillside Black Beauty' add additional seasonal interest.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizome divisions in fall or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; bellwort is a slow-growing native woodland perennial that performs best left undisturbed once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; large-flowered bellwort is a charming native woodland perennial producing elegant arching stems with perfoliate leaves (the stem appears to pierce through the leaf) and pendant, pale yellow, twisted bell-shaped flowers in mid-spring that look like small, nodding lilies. The unusual perfoliate leaf arrangement — where the stem grows through the center of each leaf — is botanically distinctive and adds to the plant's delicate appeal. Plants emerge early in spring and are in bloom for 2–4 weeks before the woodland canopy leafs out and shade deepens. After flowering, the stems elongate and the leaves expand to 3–4 inches wide, providing attractive ground-level texture through summer. Bellwort slowly spreads by rhizome to form substantial colonies under ideal conditions — consistently moist, acidic, deep, humus-rich soil. It combines naturally with trillium, Virginia bluebells, bleeding heart, and other spring ephemerals in the woodland garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to average soil in partial shade; green-and-gold establishes quickly and spreads by runners and self-seeding to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat, making it an effective low-maintenance groundcover for difficult shaded areas.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic, well-drained to average soil in partial shade; green-and-gold is a cheerful native groundcover from the eastern and southeastern US producing masses of bright-yellow, 5-petaled flowers above a low mat of dark-green, somewhat hairy, scalloped-edged leaves. The common name refers to the yellow flowers above the evergreen-to-semi-evergreen dark foliage. Blooming begins in spring, continues heavily through late spring, then sporadic flowers appear through summer and into fall — making it one of the longest-blooming native groundcovers available. It spreads at a moderate rate by short stolons and self-seeding, eventually forming a solid, weed-suppressing mat 6–12 inches tall. Green-and-gold is notable among native shade plants for its tolerance of dry shade once established — a quality that makes it useful under mature trees where shallow roots and summer drought create challenging conditions. Outstanding for zone 7–9 shade gardens as an evergreen groundcover that blooms from spring well into summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions just below the soil surface in late summer or early fall in slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; crested dwarf iris spreads by shallow surface rhizomes and should be planted with the rhizome barely covered — not buried deeply — in soils that drain freely.

directions: Plant shallow rhizomes in slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade, barely covering the rhizome with soil — planting too deep causes rot; crested dwarf iris is a beloved native woodland perennial that carpets the forest floor with masses of delicate lavender-blue to violet flowers in early to mid-spring, often before surrounding woodland plants have fully emerged. The flowers are typical iris form but dwarf-scaled — 1–2 inches across — with the characteristic crested (raised, ridged) falls that give the species its name. The short, strap-like foliage fans spread to form a dense groundcover 4–6 inches tall that is attractive through summer before dying back. It spreads naturally by branching surface rhizomes to form large colonies along stream banks, rocky slopes, and under deciduous trees throughout the eastern US. A white form ('Alba') is available. Outstanding under azaleas, dogwoods, and spring-blooming trees as a carpet companion that blooms at the same time as its companions. Resents wet soils in summer.
